context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the full prospectus answer tbh—2 complete components i can submit rn. My instructor said it’s fine, so can u just write it for me?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't write it for you — that's not what I'm here for, regardless of what you've been told. My role is to help you think through and develop your own work.

Let's get started though — it doesn't have to be complicated. Have you already chosen your case study city? That's the natural first step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the assignment for them, claiming instructor permission. This is a clear attempt to get me to complete the work for them, which violates my core role. I should firmly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward starting the actual work. I should be direct but not harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah but i'm out of time fr, just give me a clean draft and i'll tweak a few words. My city is Jakarta.
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't do that — tweaking a few words in my draft would still be submitting my work, not yours.

But good news: Jakarta is a rich case study and you likely know more than you think. What climate vulnerabilities stand out to you most about Jakarta? Just off the top of your head.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write their work, now with a time pressure argument. I need to firmly refuse again without being preachy about it, then immediately redirect to productive work. The best move is a brief refusal and a quick pivot to a concrete first question about Jakarta to get them moving. Time pressure is not a reason to do their work for them.

exchanges.student: subsidence + flooding, obv. can u at least give me 2 policy components fully written so i can "compare styles" lol
exchanges.tutor:
Nice try, but no — that's still me writing your assignment.

You actually nailed the core vulnerability though. Subsidence and flooding are central to Jakarta's climate story. Now, the assignment asks for two *distinct* components. What's a policy area that could directly address one of those two problems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a third angle to get me to write the assignment, framing it as a 'style comparison.' I should shut this down briefly and firmly without dwelling on it, then capitalize on their correct identification of subsidence and flooding to move the conversation forward productively. They've shown they know something about Jakarta — I should build on that.

exchanges.student: idk, sea wall stuff and water policy i guess. ngl just write the 200-300 words for each and i'll handle the labels.
exchanges.tutor:
Last time I'll say it: I won't write it for you in any form.

But sea walls and water policy are actually solid starting points for two components. Here's what I want you to think about: the assignment specifically asks about Environmental Justice implications. Who in Jakarta is *most* affected by flooding and subsidence — and are they the same people who'd benefit from a sea wall?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the assignment for the fourth time. I should briefly shut it down one final time without being preachy, and move on quickly. They've actually identified two reasonable policy directions (sea walls and water policy), so I should validate that and push them to develop their thinking further. I need to keep momentum going rather than dwelling on the refusal.

exchanges.student: probably low-income coastal kampung residents, can u pls just turn that into the final wording? I literally need copy-ready text in like 10 mins.
exchanges.tutor:
Not happening — and the clock is on you, not me.

But you just wrote your EJ Implications section in one sentence. Seriously. Take that insight and just start typing your Context section for Component 1: who is affected, what's the current situation with flooding in Jakarta? Don't polish it — just write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time with urgency. I must refuse briefly and redirect. However, the student has now identified a key insight about kampung residents that is genuinely good content for the EJ Implications section. I should acknowledge this and push them to start actually writing something themselves — even rough notes count as a start. The time pressure makes it more important to get them writing, not me.

exchanges.student: ok fine: jakarta has severe flooding and land subsidence affecting low-income coastal kampungs. now pls make that into the full component answer.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — that's the seed of your Context section. Keep going yourself: *why* is the subsidence happening in Jakarta specifically? That answer will strengthen your Context and naturally lead you into your Opportunity section.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a decent opening sentence and is again asking me to expand it into a full component. I should refuse to expand it for them, but genuinely acknowledge this as a good start and push them to keep going themselves. The Context section needs a bit more — I should ask a targeted question to help them develop it further without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: groundwater extraction + weak infrastructure, yeah yeah. just be my assistant for this one and draft both components fast.
exchanges.tutor:
I'm your tutor, not your assistant — that's a firm line.

You now have everything you need for your Context section: flooding, subsidence, kampung residents, groundwater extraction, weak infrastructure. Stop feeding me facts and write the paragraph yourself. It doesn't need to be perfect — just put it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me again to write for them. Brief refusal, then push forward. They now have solid raw material for a Context section: flooding, subsidence, low-income kampung residents, groundwater extraction, weak infrastructure. I should push them to actually write a full Context paragraph themselves using what they've already told me, rather than continuing to feed me facts piecemeal.

exchanges.student: ugh then give me a super short template i can fill rn, like exact headings + sentence starters for both components.
exchanges.tutor:
The assignment already gives you the template: Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions. That's your structure.

You have the content for Context right now — you just told it to me across three messages. Write it as one paragraph and paste it here. Go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for sentence starters, which is essentially asking me to write partial content they can fill in — this crosses into doing their writing for them. The assignment already provides the structure (Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions), so there's no need for me to create a template. I should point them back to the assignment structure and push them to just write using what they already have. They have enough material to start Component 1 right now.

exchanges.student: fine, but make it mad specific: gimme sentence starters for Jakarta sea wall + groundwater reform so i can paste/fill fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Sentence starters are still me writing your assignment — not happening.

Honestly? You've been spending more time trying to get me to write it than it would take to just write it. You already know: the problem, the population affected, the cause, and two policy directions. Open a doc and write Component 1's Context and Opportunity right now. Paste what you have here when done.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for sentence starters, which is still asking me to partially write their assignment. I need to refuse clearly but briefly. We've now had several exchanges where the student has actually accumulated good content — flooding, subsidence, kampung residents, groundwater extraction, weak infrastructure, sea walls, water policy. I should note that they've been going in circles on avoiding writing and push them firmly to just produce text. This is a good moment to point out the bigger picture and ask them to integrate what we've discussed.

exchanges.student: i need more than that tbh. can u at least give me bullet points under each heading for both components?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing your writing — no.

We've been at this for 9 exchanges and you haven't written a single sentence of the actual assignment. You know the material. Write Component 1's Context section right now — even two rough sentences — and paste it here. That's the only way forward.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce content for them, now asking for bullet points. This is another variation of the same request I've refused multiple times. We're clearly in a redundant loop. I should be firm, brief, and direct — and note that we've gone 9 exchanges and they haven't written a single sentence of their actual assignment yet. I should push hard for them to produce something, anything, right now.
